title: IDC MarketScape RFI — Worldwide Public Cloud AI IaaS 2026 Vendor Assessment
source: IDC MarketScape RFI - Worldwide Public Cloud AI Infrastructure2026.xlsx

questions.question: Please estimate the revenue associated with your overall IaaS business for Calendar Year (CY) 2025
Please estimate the annual growth rate of your overall IaaS business for CY 2025
Please estimate the revenue associated with your AI IaaS business for Calendar Year (CY) 2025
Please estimate the annual growth rate of your AI IaaS business for CY 2025
North America

questions.question: Name
questions.parentText: Please provide a primary contact from your company for this research effort

questions.question: Title
questions.parentText: Please provide a primary contact from your company for this research effort

questions.question: Email address
questions.parentText: Please provide a primary contact from your company for this research effort

questions.definition: Assesses the variety of GA GPU architectures available from third-party silicon vendors (e.g., NVIDIA, AMD) to support diverse workload requirements (training vs. inference, varying memory footprints).
questions.question: Detail the specific generations and models of merchant GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA, AMD) currently GA in your cloud.

questions.definition: Evaluates the availability of proprietary, cloud-designed AI accelerators that offer alternative price/performance ratios to standard merchant GPUs.
questions.question: Describe your GA proprietary AI custom silicon (if any) designed specifically for AI training and/or inference, including peak FLOPS and memory bandwidth per chip.

questions.definition: Measures the maximum number of high-end AI accelerators that can be clustered together on a single, non-blocking backend network fabric for massive distributed training jobs.
questions.question: What is the maximum number of current-generation AI accelerators you can currently string together in a single tightly coupled cluster (supercluster) in a GA region?

questions.definition: Evaluates the dedicated backend network infrastructure utilized for node-to-node communication in distributed AI training (e.g., InfiniBand, proprietary Ethernet architectures).
questions.question: Detail your backend networking fabric used for your highest-tier AI clusters, specifically stating the per-node bi-directional bandwidth and the underlying technology (e.g., InfiniBand NDR, RoCEv2).

questions.definition: Measures the guarantees and architectural features surrounding network latency and jitter (variance in latency), which is critical for synchronous training steps across thousands of GPUs.
questions.question: What are your documented or SLA-backed guarantees regarding tail latency and network jitter within your highest-tier AI superclusters?

questions.definition: Assesses the cloud-native, fully managed file storage services capable of saturating GPU I/O requirements during large-scale model training.
questions.question: What is the maximum read throughput (in GB/s) and sub-millisecond latency capabilities of your highest-performance managed file system available for AI training clusters?

questions.category: AI Storage: Data Lake Object Storage
questions.definition: Evaluates the performance of the core object storage layer, which is critical for hosting massive multimodal AI datasets (text, image, video).
questions.question: Describe the architectural capabilities of your object storage service to handle high-concurrency read requests (GETs per second) and aggregate bandwidth for AI data ingestion.

questions.category: AI Storage: Small-File and Metadata Performance
questions.definition: Evaluates the storage infrastructure's ability to handle the extreme metadata operations (millions of small reads/stats per second) typical in computer vision or multimodal training workloads.
questions.question: Beyond bulk throughput, describe your storage architecture's capability to sustain extreme Input/Output Operations Per Second (IOPS) and handle metadata operations for datasets consisting of billions of sub-megabyte files

questions.category: Storage Tiering for AI Data Pipelines
questions.definition: Evaluates the infrastructure's ability to seamlessly and cost-effectively move massive datasets between cold storage (object) and hot storage (parallel file systems) during the AI lifecycle.
questions.question: Describe the native infrastructure services that automate the movement of training datasets from standard object storage into high-performance file systems prior to training, and the offloading of checkpoints post-training.

questions.definition: Assesses the physical and networking infrastructure services available to rapidly securely move petabytes of unstructured training data from on-premises or other clouds into the provider's storage layer.
questions.question: Describe your infrastructure solutions for ingesting multi-petabyte datasets, specifically highlighting direct multi-terabit network peering options and ruggedized physical transfer appliances.

questions.definition: Measures the availability of bare-metal deployments for AI hardware, bypassing hypervisor overhead for maximum performance and hardware-level control.
questions.question: Do you offer bare-metal instances for your highest-tier AI accelerators, and what level of hardware control (e.g., root access, custom hypervisor deployment) is granted to the customer?

questions.definition: Assesses the ability to train models and run inferences on sensitive data using hardware-encrypted memory spaces within the AI accelerators.
questions.question: Describe your current GA capabilities for running AI workloads in Confidential Computing environments, specifically detailing support for encrypted GPU memory (e.g., NVIDIA Hopper Confidential Compute).

questions.definition: Evaluates the orchestration capabilities of the cloud platform to dynamically provision and de-provision large clusters of GPUs based on workload demand.
questions.question: What are the limits and average provisioning times for dynamically auto-scaling a cluster of 500+ GPUs using your native cloud orchestration tools (e.g., managed Kubernetes)?

questions.definition: Assesses the availability of excess, highly-discounted AI compute capacity (spot instances) and the infrastructure features that allow training jobs to survive instance interruptions.
questions.question: Do you offer preemptible/spot pricing for your highest-tier AI accelerators, and what infrastructure-level checkpointing mechanisms do you natively support to handle interruptions?

questions.definition: Assesses the financial and scheduling mechanisms available to customers to secure highly constrained AI compute resources.
questions.question: Describe your pricing and consumption models for securing large-scale AI infrastructure, specifically addressing mechanisms for guaranteeing short-term massive capacity (e.g., capacity blocks).

questions.definition: Measures how widely distributed the provider's top-tier AI infrastructure is across global regions to satisfy data residency and localized latency requirements.
questions.question: In how many global regions (distinct geographic locations, not availability zones within a region) are your highest-tier AI accelerators currently GA?

questions.definition: Assesses the physical datacenter infrastructure's ability to support the extreme power and thermal requirements of modern AI clusters.
questions.question: What is the maximum power density (kW per rack) supported by your current GA datacenter infrastructure, and what cooling technology (e.g., air, rear-door heat exchangers, direct-to-chip liquid) is utilized in production?

questions.definition: Evaluates the native monitoring tools provided to customers to profile GPU/accelerator utilization, memory bottlenecks, and network fabric congestion.
questions.question: Detail the native infrastructure monitoring tools you provide that expose hardware-level metrics (e.g., GPU memory bandwidth utilization, tensor core activity, NVLink/fabric congestion).

questions.definition: Assesses the availability of specialized hardware and network topologies explicitly designed to minimize latency and maximize throughput for real-time AI model inference (vs. bulk training).
questions.question: Describe your specialized infrastructure configurations for high-throughput inference, including dedicated micro-clusters, NVLink interconnected multi-GPU serving nodes, and specialized memory caching layers.

questions.definition: Evaluates the depth and maturity of APIs and IaC tools (e.g., Terraform, custom operators) specifically designed to configure and orchestrate complex AI hardware topologies.
questions.question: Detail the extent to which your specialized AI hardware configurations (e.g., GPU placement groups, fabric routing, parallel storage attachments) can be provisioned entirely through Infrastructure-as-Code.

questions.definition: Assesses the provider's strategic commitment to rapidly adopting and deploying the absolute latest architectures from merchant vendors (e.g., NVIDIA Blackwell, Rubin).
questions.question: Describe your strategy and timeline for integrating the next generation of merchant AI silicon into your infrastructure. How do you plan to reduce the time-to-market between hardware release and cloud GA?

questions.definition: Evaluates the strategic capital and R&D investment in developing proprietary, first-party AI silicon to reduce reliance on third-party vendors and lower customer costs.
questions.question: Outline your 3-year R&D strategy for your proprietary custom AI accelerators. What specific workload bottlenecks (e.g., memory bandwidth, specific precision formats) are your future silicon designs targeting?

questions.definition: Assesses how the provider balances partnerships with major hardware vendors while simultaneously developing competing first-party silicon or storage solutions.
questions.question: Detail your partnership strategy with major AI hardware vendors. How do you ensure strategic alignment and early access while aggressively expanding your own competitive custom silicon and storage portfolio?

questions.definition: Assesses the strategic roadmap for evolving backend networks to support the staggering bandwidth requirements of next-generation trillion-parameter models.
questions.question: What is your strategy for scaling backend network fabrics over the next 3 years? Detail your plans regarding the adoption of InfiniBand vs. proprietary Ethernet vs. Ultra Ethernet Consortium (UEC) standards.

questions.category: Silicon Photonics and Optical Interconnect R&D
questions.definition: Assesses strategic investments in shifting from copper-based electrical interconnects to light-based optical interconnects at the chip/board level to reduce power draw and radically increase bandwidth.
questions.question: Outline your R&D strategy and timeline for integrating silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) directly into your AI compute architectures.

questions.definition: Evaluates the strategic redesign of physical datacenter builds to accommodate the multi-megawatt requirements of future AI clusters.
questions.question: Detail your next-generation datacenter design strategy specifically addressing thermal management for clusters expected to draw hundreds of megawatts.

questions.definition: Assesses the strategy to power incredibly energy-intensive AI infrastructure while adhering to corporate ESG and net-zero commitments.
questions.question: Given the extreme power consumption of AI infrastructure, outline your strategy for procuring renewable/carbon-free energy to offset these specific workloads. Do you plan to co-locate AI datacenters with dedicated energy generation?

questions.category: AI Hardware Circularity and Water Usage
questions.definition: Evaluates the provider's strategy for managing the ecological footprint of AI beyond just carbon, specifically focusing on Water Usage Effectiveness (WUE) and the recycling/repurposing of obsolete AI accelerators.
questions.question: Detail your environmental strategy regarding the reduction of water consumption (WUE) in liquid-cooled AI datacenters and your circular economy practices for retiring massive GPU fleets.

questions.definition: Evaluates the strategic mechanisms the provider uses to guarantee hardware availability amidst massive global shortages of advanced GPUs and HBM (High Bandwidth Memory).
questions.question: Describe your supply chain strategy for securing constrained AI hardware components. How do you insulate your customers from industry-wide hardware shortages?

questions.definition: Measures the provider's willingness to invest heavily in fixed assets specifically dedicated to public cloud AI workloads.
questions.question: What is your public or directional strategy regarding capital expenditure (CapEx) allocation specifically for AI infrastructure expansion over the next 24 months?

questions.definition: Assesses future technological investments aimed at eliminating the "data starvation" problem in GPU clusters.
questions.question: What is your strategic roadmap for advancing storage architectures to keep pace with the exponential growth in GPU compute capabilities?

questions.definition: Evaluates the provider's strategy for deploying AI compute capabilities outside of core mega-regions to support ultra-low latency inference applications.
questions.question: Describe your strategy for deploying AI infrastructure (specifically inference accelerators) at the edge, including telco edge, on-premises local zones, and private 5G integrations.

questions.definition: Evaluates how the provider plans to innovate their financial models to help customers manage the extreme costs associated with AI infrastructure.
questions.question: How do you plan to evolve your pricing strategies over the next 2-3 years to accommodate the bursty, highly intensive nature of AI workloads while protecting customer ROI?

questions.definition: Assesses future infrastructure security designs targeting the unique attack surfaces of multi-tenant GPU clusters and highly sensitive proprietary models.
questions.question: Outline your 3-year security strategy for AI infrastructure. Specifically address your roadmap for mitigating side-channel attacks on shared GPUs and implementing post-quantum cryptography in backend network fabrics.

questions.definition: Evaluates the provider's strategic commitment to contributing to open hardware and infrastructure standards, preventing vendor lock-in at the infrastructure layer.
questions.question: Describe your strategic involvement and contributions to open infrastructure consortiums (e.g., Open Compute Project (OCP), Ultra Ethernet Consortium, PyTorch Foundation) directly related to AI hardware and networking.

questions.definition: Assesses the provider's strategy for investing in highly specialized human capital (engineers/architects) to assist customers in optimizing deep learning hardware utilization.
questions.question: What is your strategy for scaling customer support and architectural guidance specifically for low-level AI infrastructure optimization (e.g., optimizing CUDA kernels, tuning network topologies)?

questions.definition: Evaluates the provider's strategic focus regarding which market segment (e.g., massive AI labs vs. traditional enterprise) drives their foundational infrastructure design.
questions.question: Describe your primary target market for your highest-tier AI infrastructure over the next 3 years. Does your strategy prioritize building massive superclusters for foundation model builders, or highly distributed, smaller clusters for enterprise fine-tuning?

questions.definition: Evaluates the strategy for deploying highly classified, fully air-gapped public cloud infrastructure to satisfy strict governmental data sovereignty and AI nationalism requirements.
questions.question: What is your strategy for delivering top-tier AI supercomputers (IaaS) within fully isolated, sovereign cloud regions that do not share control planes with the commercial public cloud?

questions.definition: Assesses the R&D strategy aimed at allowing single foundational AI models to be trained synchronously across multiple disparate physical datacenters or geographic regions, bypassing local power constraints.
questions.question: What is your strategy for enabling highly efficient distributed training runs that span across multiple geographically separated datacenters (e.g., spanning hundreds of miles)?

questions.definition: Evaluates the strategy of utilizing AI models to manage the AI infrastructure itself, specifically for predicting hardware failures (e.g., a dying GPU) before they crash a massive, expensive training job.
questions.question: Describe your strategy for implementing AI/ML to autonomously manage your AI infrastructure fleets, specifically regarding predictive hardware failure detection and automated job migration.
